EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low moved from southern California eastward across the desert southwest, with southerly flow tapping abundant subtropical moisture in advance of the system. Cold low level flow did not push southward into southern NM and far west TX as strongly as anticipated, so an areawide heavy snow and ice storm episode did not materialize. However, some northern locations did receive substantial amounts of snow, while the majority of the area experienced heavy rain. Significant ice was limited to northeastern Hudspeth County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts included 6 inches at Mimbres and 9 inches in White Signal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved over southern New Mexico and far west Texas from the southwest, with moist air being lifted over a cold low level easterly flow. The result was heavy snow over the southern Sacramento Mountains as well as at most elevations in far west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from 9 inches to nearly a foot were reported in the Cloudcroft area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second storm in 4 days, this one weaker and not as moist but much colder, dropped southeastward into southern NM and far west TX. Much more snow fell out of this storm than the preceding one. Interstate 25 north of Radium Springs and Interstate 10 west of Lordsburg were closed, as were several highways in southern and southwestern NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totalled 8 inches in Hillsboro and Truth or Consequences, where travelers on Interstate 25 were sheltered.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts included 4 inches in Lordsburg and 6 inches in Animas (both are desert locations). Interstate 10 was closed west of Lordsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance was approaching a very moist and unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter measured 1.10 inches of rain within only 10 minutes, which flooded his property and the surrounding area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed within a moist and unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph downed power poles in Boles Acres. Power was knocked out in parts of nearby Alamogordo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass was in place over southern NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm dropped heavy rain and quarter size hail on the town of Weed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low moved from southern California eastward across the desert southwest, with southerly flow tapping abundant subtropical moisture in advance of the system. EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 5 inches of snow on San Augustin Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another record breaking snow event for west Texas and southeastern New Mexico as an upper level storm system and a cold front move across the region.  Six to eight inches of snow in the Van Horn area resulted in Interstate 10 being closed between Fort Hancock and Fort Stockton and Interstate 20 being closed between Pecos and Fort Hancock.  At least 200 tractor trailers were stranded in Van Horn and Pecos when those interstates closed.  Schools across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico closed for at least a day.  Locations across the region received anywhere from an inch to eleven inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven inches of snow fell at Queen, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms moved eastward across southern New Mexico during the predawn hours. At least one of the storms was severe.

A broad trough of low pressure crossed New Mexico in two stages, bringing periods of heavy snow to northern and western New Mexico.  Around 4 inches of snow fell between 6500 and 7500 feet with 6 to 12 inches common above 7500 feet.

A broad trough of low pressure crossed New Mexico in two stages, bringing periods of heavy snow to northern and western New Mexico.  Around 4 inches of snow fell between 6500 and 7500 feet with 6 to 12 inches common above 7500 feet.

A vigorous upper low moved southeast across New Mexico while a strong cold front plunged south across the eastern plains.  A narrow band of moderate to heavy snow and blowing snow developed during the evening of the 25th, with snowfall of 2 to 5 inches and reduced visibility in blowing snow.  Numerous vehicles went off the roadway along Interstate 40 between Clines Corners and Tucumcari.  Heavy snow was also reported in Fort Sumner.

A large, slow moving upper low pressure system with abundant moisture brought heavy snowfall to the mountains of southwestern New Mexico. Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches above 7000 feet north of Silver City were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to ten inches of snow fell in the mountains of extreme northern Grant County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy snow of 4 to 7 inches was reported across the mountain communities of north central New Mexico as an upper level system passed quickly overhead.

A strong Pacific trough moved across New Mexico, dumping heavy snow mainly in favored upslope areas. Six inches of snow fell in Mimbres (about 6000 feet ASL), with much heavier amounts above the western slopes of the southern Sacramento Mountains (westerly flow). Up to 12 inches fell in Mescalero, and numerous roads were closed in the Cloudcroft area.

Six inches of snow fell in Mimbres (about 6000 feet ASL), with much heavier amounts above the western slopes of the southern Sacramento Mountains (westerly flow). Up to 12 inches fell in Mescalero, and numerous roads were closed in the Cloudcroft area.

A broad upper trough over the southwestern U.S. with tight pressure gradients through a deep layer, along with a strong upper jet over New Mexico and a deepening surface low in the Texas panhandle resulted in strong winds in southern NM and far west TX. Sustained winds were more than 40 mph in some locations with gusts to near 60 mph.
Another period of strong downslope winds and gustiness from the central mountains east across the plains of eastern New Mexico.

An unusually early and intense outbreak of severe storms with large hail and tornadoes occurred across east central and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 23rd. The average date for isolated first reports of damaging hail over the past 20 years has been March 24th with the location typically confined to far southeast New Mexico. The March 23rd 2007 episode produced a number of large hail events from Roswell to Tucumcari and peaked with multiple tornadoes from near Tatum north to Clovis and northeast of Tucumcari. A tornado at Clovis resulted in the death of two elderly citizens, the first tornado fatalities in New Mexico since October of 1974. The episode was characterized by a slow moving upper level low that produced strong speed shear but nearly uniform southerly directional flow aloft across the eastern and southeastern sections of the state. Storms with large hail developed first during early and mid afternoon from near Roswell north to Tucumcari. Towards late afternoon and early evening as storms migrated north northeast they encountered an increasing but shallow easterly surface flow that enhanced low level shear resulting in brief but shallow tornadoes. Multiple small but elevated vortices were observed circulating around well defined wall clouds with occasional spin downs into brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail over Dexter as storm moves north.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small and possibly elevated landspout type tornado developed on the far east end of a rear flank outflow that swung south through east over Logan as a storm centered about 5 miles to the west moved north over Ute Lake. Damage along the track as it moved over the west side of Logan was intermittent with damage indicators pointing to winds of general 80 to 90 mph with peak winds near the south and north ends of the track estimated at 105 mph. Average track width was around 100 yards. Logan had no tornado siren system but citizens reported nearly 30 to 50 minutes notice of an approaching severe thunderstorm. About a dozen injuries were reported with only two transported to Tucumcari for brief treatment in hospital. Heaviest damage was mainly confined to about 50 manufactured homes and recreational vehicles tossed and rolled mainly on the south end of the lake resort community. Damage to site constructed homes and buildings was limited to window, roof and soffit damage except for one 1910 era frame home that lost its roof.

An upper disturbance rotating around the backside of an upper low located over west Texas triggered southward-moving thunderstorms over south central New Mexico. Dry air in the sub-cloud layer contributed to at least one case of severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several gusts from 60 mph to 65 mph were estimated during this thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow from a huge thunderstorm complex about 250 miles to the east pushed a dry line westward beyond the Rio Grande in southern NM. This was the focus for strong thunderstorms the afternoon of the 28th.
Strong winds knocked down trees and power lines in Roswell and damaged roofs.

A strong south to southeast flow brought low level moisture into New Mexico, while an upper level disturbance crossed the state. This produced large hail over portions of central and south central New Mexico.

A strong south to southeast flow brought low level moisture into New Mexico, while an upper level disturbance crossed the state. This produced large hail over portions of central and south central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reported by EMT north of Alamo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 327pm and 415pm MST there were 3 reports of large hail and 2 reports of funnel clouds around Encino. Hail size ranged from 0.75 inch to 1.75 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist atmosphere and numerous outflow boundaries help set off a couple of severe thunderstorms, one over Roswell and the other in Portales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail on West 2nd street in Roswell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist atmosphere and numerous outflow boundaries help set off a couple of severe thunderstorms, one over Roswell and the other in Portales.
Continued deep low level moisture over the eastern plains, combined with a  moderate upper level disturbance, kicked off another round of thunderstorms, with penny size hail reported in Union County.
A cold front approaching northeast New Mexico on the 29th help trigger isolated thunderstorms. One inch hail was observed in Grenville.

Moist southeast flow at the lower levels pushed westward into far west Texas and southern New Mexico under a late season southwest flow aloft. The resulting veering shear profile was favorable for severe thunderstorms, more so in the Texas portion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell south of Radium Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storm moved from north to south 2 miles SSE of Grants, right over the NW NM visitor center.
Strong winds knocked down trees and power lines in Roswell and damaged roofs.

An upper level storm system moved east across northern Mexico across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Ahead of this system, a moist and unstable airmass was in place. As temperatures warmed throughout the day, the atmosphere became more unstable. Storms initiated in the Big Bend and across the mountains of southeastern New Mexico and moved north and eastward. |The Midland county warning area saw five recorded tornadoes on the 23rd, four in New Mexico and one in Texas. The four in New Mexico were documented by both trained Skywarn spotters and by a National Weather Service damage survey team. The tornado in Texas was recorded by a television news/weather crew in the area. Baseball size hail was also associated with the Texas storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to golfball size hail fell in and around Carlsbad.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms trained across Lea County for much of the night and produced flash flooding that was evident to NWS Damage Survey team members the following day.  Several miles west of Tatum, team members discovered a three to four foot deep lake that is normally open field.  In and around Lovington, there were locations that had running water alongside the road 18 hours after the last storm had moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable air mass within moist summer regime.
A weak upper disturbance moved into a very moist an unstable air mass over southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some of the roads from Boles Acres (south of Alamogordo) to Dog Canyon were under water. Dog Canyon Road was temporarily closed to due flooded low water crossings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms within a very moist air mass resulted in very heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 30 homes were flooded in Anthony. Sandbags were requested for this area as well as nearby Mesquite (just to the north).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass settled over southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 9 was closed from Columbus east due to flooding. Parts of town were also flooded, becoming worse southward toward the international boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved slowly across the desert southwest. Enough moisture was present for heavy snowfall in the uplope areas of the southern Sacramento Mountains. Amounts around 10 inches were common from Cloudcroft to Sunspot, with isolated reports of 13 inches.
An isolated strong wind event occurred over Quay and Lincoln counties.
An isolated strong wind event occurred over Quay and Lincoln counties.
Strong wind gusts were observed around Placitas, in far southeast Sandoval County.

A strong back door cold front plunged south across the eastern plains during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th. The fresh supply of moisture behind this front helped spark numerous showers and thunderstorms  over eastern New Mexico. The storms began in the early afternoon over the northeast, and advanced south later in the afternoon and evening. The severe weather was initially in the form of large hail, but evolved into a significant high wind event for east central and southeast portions of the state, as an outflow boundary raced south ahead of the main line of thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent heavy precipitation supercell moved northward into the NM Bootheel along the Peloncillo Mountains. A strong wet microburst blew large hail nearly horizontally and resulted in extensive hail damage to ranches in that area.

A severe thunderstorm moved slowly southward through the Mimbres Valley and released a substantial amount of large hail on and around the town of Mimbres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, mainly quarter to pin pong ball size, accumulated to a depth of at least a foot in Mimbres. The sound that accompanied the hail shaft while still in the distance was described as a roar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surge of low level moisture moved into the state late on the 8th and early on the 9th, thanks in part to a strong back door cold front. The front moved south across the easter plains on the 8th and rushed through the central mountain passes into the Rio Grande Valley early on the 9th. The moisture pressed all the way back to the continental divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail broke windshields of several cars on U.S. Highway 82.

An upper low moved from southern AZ across southern NM and far west TX over an air mass that was rather moist for early May in this area. The air mass was very unstable, such that any ground based circulation (e.g. gustnadoes) would quickly spin up within strong updrafts. One technician near White Sands Missle Range HQ observed a gustnado along a gust front from a storm farther west grow rapidly upward under a strong thunderstorm. Once the landspout had formed, it moved slowly northeast but persisted for more than 40 minutes (52 minutes according to NSSL). It remained over unpopulated desert terrain and thus no damage occurred. The tornado lasted far longer than one would expect for a landspout. It was concluded that since it connected with a cell that had supercellular tendencies, this accounted for the longevity. Due to the somewhat high thunderstorm base and little precipitation around the funnel, the tornado was easily viewed by El Paso residents up to 30 miles away. Several other severe cells developed that afternoon, with another weak tornado briefly touching down about 25 miles to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The landspout tornado which originated in Dona County crossed into Otero County. Though its duration was longer in this county, the path was shorter due to much slower movement.

A strong back door cold front plunged south across the eastern plains during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th. The fresh supply of moisture behind this front helped spark numerous showers and thunderstorms  over eastern New Mexico. The storms began in the early afternoon over the northeast, and advanced south later in the afternoon and evening. The severe weather was initially in the form of large hail, but evolved into a significant high wind event for east central and southeast portions of the state, as an outflow boundary raced south ahead of the main line of thunderstorms.

An upper low moved from southern AZ across southern NM and far west TX over an air mass that was rather moist for early May in this area. The air mass was very unstable, such that any ground based circulation (e.g. gustnadoes) would quickly spin up within strong updrafts. One technician near White Sands Missle Range HQ observed a gustnado along a gust front from a storm farther west grow rapidly upward under a strong thunderstorm. Once the landspout had formed, it moved slowly northeast but persisted for more than 40 minutes (52 minutes according to NSSL). It remained over unpopulated desert terrain and thus no damage occurred. The tornado lasted far longer than one would expect for a landspout. It was concluded that since it connected with a cell that had supercellular tendencies, this accounted for the longevity. Due to the somewhat high thunderstorm base and little precipitation around the funnel, the tornado was easily viewed by El Paso residents up to 30 miles away. Several other severe cells developed that afternoon, with another weak tornado briefly touching down about 25 miles to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado briefly touched down in open desert south of U.S. 70 in extreme western Otero County.

Another cold front and upper level storm system coincided over west Texas and southeastern New Mexico to produce another round of winter weather.  Snow, sleet, and freezing rain all occurred across the region making traveling hazardous and keeping students hoping for the second snow day in a week.  Numerous accidents were reported across the area, especially overnight when precipitation that had melted during the day re-froze on the roads.  This led to several roads being closed overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell in Tatum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low moved from southern California eastward across the desert southwest, with southerly flow tapping abundant subtropical moisture in advance of the system. Cold low level flow did not push southward into southern NM and far west TX as strongly as anticipated, so an areawide heavy snow and ice storm episode did not materialize. However, some northern locations did receive substantial amounts of snow, while the majority of the area experienced heavy rain. Significant ice was limited to northeastern Hudspeth County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow fell in in Hillsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and associated strong subtropical jet brought heavy rain, high elevation snow, severe thunderstorms and high winds to western and central New Mexico during the predawn hours of December 1st.  Flooding was observed in the Gila region while severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in the Rio Grande and Estancia Valleys. Strong winds blew across much of the high terrain and just east of the central mountain chain but were the strongest during the daylight hours of the 1st. The winds were occasionally accompanied by snow, which created near blizzard conditions in the higher mountainous terrain. As the trough opened up and moved east across New Mexico and Colorado, snow levels lowered overnight across the northwest.  However, the most significant snow accumulations were confined to elevations above 8500 feet in the Chuska, Southern San Juan and Sangre De Cristo Mountains of northern New Mexico. Through dawn of the 2nd, storm total snow accumulations of 5 to 10 inches were common with 1 to 2 feet likely on west facing slopes above 9500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 53 knots was recorded at Double Eagle Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed within a moist and unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from nickel to quarter size fell at the Sacramento Post Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second storm in 4 days, this one weaker and not as moist but much colder, dropped southeastward into southern NM and far west TX. Much more snow fell out of this storm than the preceding one. Interstate 25 north of Radium Springs and Interstate 10 west of Lordsburg were closed, as were several highways in southern and southwestern NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 8 to 10 inches of snow fell around Silver City, 13 inches in Pinos Altos, 10 inches in Kingston and 8 inches at Lake Roberts. Buckhorn, a desert location received 7 inches. Most roads were closed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system passed over northern New Mexico and the panhandle of Texas into Oklahoma. This substantially tightened the pressure gradients over west Texas and southeastern New Mexico, resulting in strong to severe straight line winds across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS cooperative observer in Tatum recorded sustained windspeeds up to 49 mph and gusting to 65 mph.

A weak shortwave progressing through upper level southerly flow combined with instability along an eastward mixing dryline to produce severe thunderstorms across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. It was also unstable, resulting in heavy convective snow showers and snowfall rates of up to 3 inches per hour in the southern Sacramento Mountains. A total of 14 inches of snow fell in Cloudcroft.

The combination of strong over-running and extended periods of easterly upslope winds produced heavy snow across central and eastern New Mexico as a cold arctic air mass covered the region and small disturbances passed overhead. Cold air eventually spread west through the central valleys into the western mountains to generate snow from slow moving upper level disturbances. East central counties saw the heaviest and longest duration snow with amounts topping 10 inches at Melrose in Curry County. Other surrounding areas between Clovis, Fort Sumner and Roswell reported 5 to 7 inches. Central mountain areas from east of Albuquerque south to Ruidoso saw amounts of 6 to 10 inches. Three to five inches snow fell across portions of the Middle Rio Grande Valley while the peak snow amounts in the western areas of the state included 7 to 8 inches as far west as the Four Corners region and the plateaus between Gallup and Socorro.

Another cold front and upper level storm system coincided over west Texas and southeastern New Mexico to produce another round of winter weather.  Snow, sleet, and freezing rain all occurred across the region making traveling hazardous and keeping students hoping for the second snow day in a week.  Numerous accidents were reported across the area, especially overnight when precipitation that had melted during the day re-froze on the roads.  This led to several roads being closed overnight.

A storm over northern California on the 11th moved slowly over New Mexico by the 13th, bringing cold temperatures and plenty of moisture. This resulted in widespread snow over northern New Mexico from late on the 12th into the early morning hours of the 14th. Snow amounts were in the 5 to 7 inch range over the Sangre De Cristo and Sandia Mountains, while 6 to 10 inches fell over parts of the Northeast Highlands, and 8 to 16 inches in a few spots of the Northwest Mountains.

Around 12:30 pm MDT on Friday, March 23, thunderstorms began to develop over the Guadalupe Mountains of Eddy County.  Thunderstorms continued to rapidly develop, becoming severe over portions of Eddy County at 1:45 pm MDT.  Thunderstorms also were developing over eastern portions of Culberson County during this time and these thunderstorms would eventually affect Lea County later in the afternoon.|At 3:39 pm MDT, a thunderstorm located in western portions of Lea County just northwest of the community of Halfway began to exhibit supercellular characteristics.  This prompted the issuance of a Severe Thunderstorm Warning at 3:42 pm MDT for western portions of Lea county.  As the supercell thunderstorm moved northeast at 30 mph, the storm continued to intensify.  The presence on radar of an intensifying mesocyclone (storm rotation) and a bounded weak echo region, along with key spotter information relayed into our office through amateur radio Net Control Operations prompted an upgrade to a tornado warning.  Spotters indicated a developing wall cloud over the area had become fully formed with increasing surface wind inflow.  A tornado warning was issued for northern portions of Lea County at 4:28 pm MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fourth tornado was witnessed three miles southwest of Crossroads where it destroyed a 10' x 10' oil field pump house.  This tornado remained over open country for the most part and was assigned an F0 rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually early and intense outbreak of severe storms with large hail and tornadoes occurred across east central and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 23rd. The average date for isolated first reports of damaging hail over the past 20 years has been March 24th with the location typically confined to far southeast New Mexico. The March 23rd 2007 episode produced a number of large hail events from Roswell to Tucumcari and peaked with multiple tornadoes from near Tatum north to Clovis and northeast of Tucumcari. A tornado at Clovis resulted in the death of two elderly citizens, the first tornado fatalities in New Mexico since October of 1974. The episode was characterized by a slow moving upper level low that produced strong speed shear but nearly uniform southerly directional flow aloft across the eastern and southeastern sections of the state. Storms with large hail developed first during early and mid afternoon from near Roswell north to Tucumcari. Towards late afternoon and early evening as storms migrated north northeast they encountered an increasing but shallow easterly surface flow that enhanced low level shear resulting in brief but shallow tornadoes. Multiple small but elevated vortices were observed circulating around well defined wall clouds with occasional spin downs into brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado that developed in Roosevelt County continued north northwest into Curry County for about 3.5 miles then tracked north northeast an additional 4.6 miles into southern and east central sections of Clovis. Intensity of the tornado appeared to wane from EF2 120 mph in Roosevelt County to EF1 100 mph as it moved north into Curry County along Highway 70 where damage was limited to power lines and farm irrigation equipment. Intensity increased again to EF2 level 125 mph for a segment extending from about 4 miles south of Clovis northward into southeast Clovis which sustained the heaviest and most consistent damage as indicated by structural damage and downed power poles. The tornado appeared to wane again as it move north over Highway 60/84 just east of the intersection with Highway 70. The tornado track became intermittent north of Highway 60/84 with winds likely less than 85 mph before a final one half mile track of heavier damage and EF2 level winds of 120-125 mph. Average width was estimated at 200 yards. About 500 homes and other facilities sustained at least some damage ranging from complete destruction of mobile homes in southeast Clovis and wall collapse at several businesses along Highway 60/84 to the loss of roof shingles and roof top air conditioning units. Thirty five people suffered treatment injuries including five that required hospitalization. Two elderly citizens died later from injuries sustained during the event making these the first tornado fatalities in New Mexico since October of 1974.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued deep moisture and instability over the eastern plains of New Mexico resulted in strong thunderstorms with heavy rain over portions of the eastern plains. An outflow boundary from a thunderstorm in Quay county produced a wind gust to 60 mph.

As the supercell thunderstorm moved northeast at 30 mph, the storm continued to intensify.  The presence on radar of an intensifying mesocyclone (storm rotation) and a bounded weak echo region, along with key spotter information relayed into our office through amateur radio Net Control Operations prompted an upgrade to a tornado warning.  Spotters indicated a developing wall cloud over the area had become fully formed with increasing surface wind inflow.  A tornado warning was issued for northern portions of Lea County at 4:28 pm MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:50 pm MDT, spotters indicated the presence of two wall clouds.  Primary indications are that the new wall cloud spawned a second tornado with the supercell thunderstorm 7 1/2 miles west of the community of McDonald, just south of Lea County Road 147.  Survey of damage along county road 147 led to an estimation of tornado width of 50 yards at this point. |The tornado continued to intensify as it moved northeast.  Significant damage was seen 7 1/2 miles southwest of Tatum over ranchland.  At this location, 13 wooden power poles were damaged, three snapped off at ground level and others snapped three to twelve feet above ground.  A water trough weighing an estimated 300 pounds was thrown from the tornado for several hundred feet.  The tornado was visually estimated to be a half mile in width and described as clean in appearance.  Damage along this track supports this estimate.  It is believed that this tornado lifted just south of N.M. Highway 380, six and a half miles west of Tatum.  Spotters also confirmed that the tornado exhibited multiple vortex structure at times during its life cycle.

An upper level low pressure system located over northern portions of Mexico in combination with east to southeast surface winds pulling in abundant amounts of Gulf moisture and providing upslope flow, led to the development of showers and thunderstorms across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. |During the overnight hours on early Sunday morning, showers and thunderstorms moved out of northern Mexico into southwest Texas. Additional thunderstorms developed over the higher elevations of southwest Texas leading to pre-sunrise severe weather in the form of hail and flash flooding across the southwest portions of the County Warning Area. As the morning progressed, a large area of embedded showers and thunderstorms moved northeastward across southeastern New Mexico and the Texas Permian Basin producing occasional severe weather, before exiting into the South Plains of West Texas. |With antecedent conditions remaining in place, the additional boundaries left by morning convection and daytime heating led to thunderstorm re-development by early Sunday afternoon and a significant severe weather event that affected much of the area into the pre-dawn hours on Monday morning.

An upper level storm system moved east across northern Mexico across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Ahead of this system, a moist and unstable airmass was in place. As temperatures warmed throughout the day, the atmosphere became more unstable. Storms initiated in the Big Bend and across the mountains of southeastern New Mexico and moved north and eastward. |The Midland county warning area saw five recorded tornadoes on the 23rd, four in New Mexico and one in Texas. The four in New Mexico were documented by both trained Skywarn spotters and by a National Weather Service damage survey team. The tornado in Texas was recorded by a television news/weather crew in the area. Baseball size hail was also associated with the Texas storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half-dollar size hail fell west and northwest of Carlsbad.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball sized hail fell in Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft, unseasonably warm temperatures, and the development of a dryline combined to produce showers and thunderstorms across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.

A shortwave trough moved through the subtropical jet stream into west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.  This combined with moderate levels of CAPE and deep layer shear to produce supercell thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported at Western Bar & Grill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved southeast across the Eastern Plains of New Mexico during the late afternoon and produced penny to quarter size hail.

Deep moisture and a weak upper level disturbance combined to produce a severe thunderstorm over southwest Chaves county, at the intersections of U.S. Highway 82 and state route 13, or about 13 miles ENE of Dunken.
Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which caused street flooding between 1 and 2 feet deep at the intersection of Cerillos Road and Camino Carlos Rey. Cars were stalled in the intersection. A section of Rodeo Road was closed due to flooding.  Two homes on the east side of town experienced flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The reports from motorists were not near the core of a supercell thunderstorm, implying larger hail unreported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Border Patrol checkpoint on U.S. 70 southwest of Alamogordo passed along several reports of penny size hail from westbound motorists.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved from southern AZ across southern NM and far west TX over an air mass that was rather moist for early May in this area. The air mass was very unstable, such that any ground based circulation (e.g. gustnadoes) would quickly spin up within strong updrafts. One technician near White Sands Missle Range HQ observed a gustnado along a gust front from a storm farther west grow rapidly upward under a strong thunderstorm. Once the landspout had formed, it moved slowly northeast but persisted for more than 40 minutes (52 minutes according to NSSL). It remained over unpopulated desert terrain and thus no damage occurred. The tornado lasted far longer than one would expect for a landspout. It was concluded that since it connected with a cell that had supercellular tendencies, this accounted for the longevity. Due to the somewhat high thunderstorm base and little precipitation around the funnel, the tornado was easily viewed by El Paso residents up to 30 miles away. Several other severe cells developed that afternoon, with another weak tornado briefly touching down about 25 miles to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually long lasting and large landspout developed under a supercell. It remained over open terrain, so no damage or injuries resulted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent heavy precipitation supercell moved northward into the NM Bootheel along the Peloncillo Mountains. A strong wet microburst blew large hail nearly horizontally and resulted in extensive hail damage to ranches in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds gusting to an estimated 80 mph drove a large amount of quarter size hail into the sides of buildings on the Midbar Ranch. All screens on the windward side were destroyed. Bark was stripped from trees on the windward side of trunks. Tall grass was stripped to near the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly intensified over the Sangre De Cristos and produced a quick burst of large hail.
Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail across far northeast New Mexico.
Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail across far northeast New Mexico.
Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in a localized area just south of Jemez Springs. This caused flooding and mudslides across four sections of State Highway 4 causing the road to be temporarily closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail across far northeast New Mexico.
Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail across far northeast New Mexico.
Deep moisture and strong instability led to an isolated report of large hail in San Miguel county on the 11th.

Around 12:30 pm MDT on Friday, March 23, thunderstorms began to develop over the Guadalupe Mountains of Eddy County.  Thunderstorms continued to rapidly develop, becoming severe over portions of Eddy County at 1:45 pm MDT.  Thunderstorms also were developing over eastern portions of Culberson County during this time and these thunderstorms would eventually affect Lea County later in the afternoon.|At 3:39 pm MDT, a thunderstorm located in western portions of Lea County just northwest of the community of Halfway began to exhibit supercellular characteristics.  This prompted the issuance of a Severe Thunderstorm Warning at 3:42 pm MDT for western portions of Lea county.  As the supercell thunderstorm moved northeast at 30 mph, the storm continued to intensify.  The presence on radar of an intensifying mesocyclone (storm rotation) and a bounded weak echo region, along with key spotter information relayed into our office through amateur radio Net Control Operations prompted an upgrade to a tornado warning.  Spotters indicated a developing wall cloud over the area had become fully formed with increasing surface wind inflow.  A tornado warning was issued for northern portions of Lea County at 4:28 pm MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:32 pm MDT, a trained Skywarn spotter, reported a tornado touchdown with a small debris cloud approximately 12 miles WSW of Lovington.  At 4:36 pm MDT, trained spotters viewed the same tornado from N.M. Highway 457 looking southward.  Damage survey interviews with officials and residents revealed that the brief tornado remained over open pasture lands.  An EF0 rating was assigned to this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually early and intense outbreak of severe storms with large hail and tornadoes occurred across east central and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 23rd. The average date for isolated first reports of damaging hail over the past 20 years has been March 24th with the location typically confined to far southeast New Mexico. The March 23rd 2007 episode produced a number of large hail events from Roswell to Tucumcari and peaked with multiple tornadoes from near Tatum north to Clovis and northeast of Tucumcari. A tornado at Clovis resulted in the death of two elderly citizens, the first tornado fatalities in New Mexico since October of 1974. The episode was characterized by a slow moving upper level low that produced strong speed shear but nearly uniform southerly directional flow aloft across the eastern and southeastern sections of the state. Storms with large hail developed first during early and mid afternoon from near Roswell north to Tucumcari. Towards late afternoon and early evening as storms migrated north northeast they encountered an increasing but shallow easterly surface flow that enhanced low level shear resulting in brief but shallow tornadoes. Multiple small but elevated vortices were observed circulating around well defined wall clouds with occasional spin downs into brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado with estimated peak winds of near 120 mph severely damaged the Portales City water well facility on State Road 202 about 5 miles east of Highway 70. The tornado moved north northwest destroying a new dairy before continuing northwest into Curry County. No injuries reported but storm debris briefly trapped a dairy employee. About 190 dairy cows either killed by the event or else sent to immediate slaughter due to injuries. Average width approximately 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced up to 2.5 inches of rain in the foothills and mountains just east of Santa Fe. Runoff into arroyos flooded 3 nearby homes. One house had water up to a foot deep in it.  Runoff forced the closure of Cerro Gordo Road.

An upper level closed low, a shortwave trough, and a low level southeasterly moisture fetch all converged over southeastern New Mexico and west Texas. All of this led to an organized Mesoscale Convective System that moved across the area dumping one to three inches of rain and resulting in massive flash flooding in Ector County. While the main threat, and most of the resulting damage was due to flash flooding, moderate to strong CAPE existed across the area and produced up to softball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail piled to two inches deep on New Mexico State Highway 31, four to five miles south of U.S. Highway 62/180.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail fell at the intersection of Coleman Road and U.S. Highway 62/180 south of Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level ridge was oriented southwest/northeast from the eastern Pacific Ocean into the central U.S. plains. A persistent shear axis remained entrenched over much of central and eastern Texas. West Texas and southeastern New Mexico was sandwiched between these two features several days prior to June 23rd. At the surface, a trough situated from the Texas Panhandle extended west-southwestward into southwestern New Mexico, then south roughly along the Rio Grande River. ||An upper level disturbance moved south between the aforementioned upper level features during the afternoon into a moderately unstable airmass. Thunderstorms developed in the afternoon over the higher terrain of eastern New Mexico and west Texas, and along the surface trough over the Texas Panhandle. Convection organized into a linear Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) by that evening over eastern New Mexico as it moved southeastward into a low level jet. The MCS began to bow southeastward as it moved into the southeastern New Mexico plains. Wind gusts as high as 61 kt were reported over Lea county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Carlsbad (KCNM) recorded multiple wind gusts of 59 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall associated with a frontal boundary and weak upper level disturbance moved into the forecast area causing widespread severe weather and isolated flooding. A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was issued at 6:10 PM CDT and remained in effect until 1 AM CDT and was then extended until 5 AM as the line continued to push south. Damage reports began reaching the office around 9:30 PM CDT (8:30 MDT) as the line entered the northwestern portion of the forecast area and continued until 3:30 AM CDT when the line exited the forecast area. ||As the storm entered Lea County New Mexico, reports of wind speeds in excess of 70 mph began coming into the office. These high winds continued as the line surged forward into West Texas. In Seminole, 98 mph (measured) wind speeds toppled several radio towers including a 320 foot tower that fell on a church and bus damaging both. In addition, the roof of Beall's Department store was destroyed and two mobile homes were rolled and destroyed injuring four people. ||As the line continued its southward progression, so did the damaging winds. Many locations began experiencing power outages including two of the three television stations in the Midland/Odessa area, and Midland International Airport. WFO Midland and the Gaines County Emergency Operation Center continued working on back up generator power until commercial power was restored to the area. A 93 mph wind gust was measured at Midland International Airport just prior to the power loss, which rendered it inoperable for the remainder of the night. ||The event continued into the early morning hours on Wednesday, where Dryden reported a 63 mph wind gust prior to thunderstorms exiting the area. ||Post storm damage surveys indicated that damage across the area was caused by a derecho. A derecho is a ���widespread and long-lived wind storm associated with a band of rapidly moving showers or thunderstorms.��� ||While straight-line winds are common across West Texas ��� especially during the late spring and early summer ��� the magnitude of the wind speeds for this event is quite rare. As an example, the 93 mph wind reported in Midland eclipsed the old record of 86 mph set in February 1960, and was the strongest wind ever recorded in Midland since the NWS began official record keeping in 1930. ||During this long-lived weather episode, all but two of the 26 counties within WFO Midland���s CWA experienced severe weather. The majority of these locations experienced high wind events, which resulted in power outages across the area. Because of backup power capability, WFO Midland was able to maintain warning operations throughout the entire severe weather event. ||Summary of measured wind reports: ||Dryden (K6R6) - 55 kts (63 mph) |Big Spring (KBPG) - 51 kts (59 mph) |Fort Stockton (KFST) - 57 kts (66 mph) |Guadalupe Pass (KGDP) - 62 kts (71 mph) |Wink (KINK) - 58 kts (67 mph) |Midland (KMAF) - 81 kts (93 mph)* |Odessa (KODO) - 55 kts (63 mph) |Seagraves 1 SW (KSGV) - 66 kts (76 mph) |Andrews 2 E (KANS) - 51 kts (59 mph)* |Seminole 2 NNE (KSMS) - 85 kts (98 mph)* |Lamesa 2 SE (KLES) - 45 kts (52 mph)* |Gail 2 ESE (KGGS) - 43 kts (50 mph)* |Pecos (KPEQ) - 56 kts (64 mph) |McDonald Observatory - 61 kts (70 mph) |Tatum, NM Co-op Observer - 68 kts (78 mph) ||*These sites lost power shortly after observations, it may be assumed that peak wind gusts were somewhat higher than observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in Artesia reported winds gusting to 60 mph.  These winds blew around lawn furniture and damaged a residential porch.

Quarter sized hail fell in Lakewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moist southeasterly flow under a stronger southwest flow aloft created an environment favorable for severe thunderstorms in southwestern New Mexico during a span of two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down (one of them uprooted) and 2 power poles knocked over near Peru Mill at the northwest edge of Deming. Low level moisture had retreated further east resulting in a dry microburst from a high based thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cool pockets of air aloft dropping down the front side of an upper high moved over a moist air mass and triggered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to ping pong size accumulated to a 2 inch depth about 2 miles southeast of Cloudcroft. The majority of the hailstones were nickel size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cool pockets of air aloft dropping down the front side of an upper high moved over a moist air mass and triggered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small rope-like tornado was reported by weather observers at Holloman AFB. It was observed for 8 minutes over the Sacramento Mountains beneath a thunderstorm about 3 miles southeast of Cloudcroft. The tornado was nearly stationary while the thunderstorm moved south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with weak rotation developed in a moist and sheared environment. Ping-pong ball size hail fell between Weed and Pinon NM in the Sacramento Mountains.
Low level moist southeasterly flow under a stronger southwest flow aloft created an environment favorable for severe thunderstorms in southwestern New Mexico during a span of two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was driven by strong thunderstorm winds and damaged roofs, screens and doors along the 2200 block of Route 418 southwest of Deming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moist southeasterly flow under a stronger southwest flow aloft created an environment favorable for severe thunderstorms in southwestern New Mexico during a span of two days.

A strong back door cold front plunged south across the eastern plains during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th. The fresh supply of moisture behind this front helped spark numerous showers and thunderstorms  over eastern New Mexico. The storms began in the early afternoon over the northeast, and advanced south later in the afternoon and evening. The severe weather was initially in the form of large hail, but evolved into a significant high wind event for east central and southeast portions of the state, as an outflow boundary raced south ahead of the main line of thunderstorms.

An upper level closed low, a shortwave trough, and a low level southeasterly moisture fetch all converged over southeastern New Mexico and west Texas. All of this led to an organized Mesoscale Convective System that moved across the area dumping one to three inches of rain and resulting in massive flash flooding in Ector County. While the main threat, and most of the resulting damage was due to flash flooding, moderate to strong CAPE existed across the area and produced up to softball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water in Rocky Arroyo flooded the intersection of New Mexico State Highway 137 and U.S. Highway 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which resulted in flooding of several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home was initially flooded in Zuni as a result of heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level closed low, a shortwave trough, and a low level southeasterly moisture fetch all converged over southeastern New Mexico and west Texas. All of this led to an organized Mesoscale Convective System that moved across the area dumping one to three inches of rain and resulting in massive flash flooding in Ector County. While the main threat, and most of the resulting damage was due to flash flooding, moderate to strong CAPE existed across the area and produced up to softball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water flowed over New Mexico Highway 18 and County Road 78.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved through the subtropical jet stream into west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.  This combined with moderate levels of CAPE and deep layer shear to produce supercell thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell on Highway 380, six miles west of Tatum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass in place over much of the area, daytime heating, and an upper level disturbance over west Texas combined to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. The majority of this activity occurred across the Permian Basin and southeastern New Mexico, with rainfall totals exceeding four inches in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported one foot of running water on many streets in Carlsbad. Local law enforcement reported two to three vehicles stalled in high water at the intersection of Canal and Tansil streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass in place over much of the area, daytime heating, and an upper level disturbance over west Texas combined to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. The majority of this activity occurred across the Permian Basin and southeastern New Mexico, with rainfall totals exceeding four inches in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four foot deep water flowed over Dark Canyon Road two miles west of Hidalgo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating, upslope flow, and a weak upper level disturbance moved across southeastern New Mexico and the northern Permian Basin and combined to produce scattered thunderstorms. A few storms moved at a slow pace, resulting in flash flooding of urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding became widespread beginning on the north side of Hobbs and then expanded to the south side of the city. One foot of water covered the intersection of Grimes and Joe Harvey streets. Cars were stalled at the intersection of Joe Harvey and New Mexico State Highway 118 as a result of over two feet of water covering the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating, upslope flow, and a weak upper level disturbance moved across southeastern New Mexico and the northern Permian Basin and combined to produce scattered thunderstorms. A few storms moved at a slow pace, resulting in flash flooding of urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to golf ball sized hail fell north of Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating, upslope flow, and a weak upper level disturbance moved across southeastern New Mexico and the northern Permian Basin and combined to produce scattered thunderstorms. A few storms moved at a slow pace, resulting in flash flooding of urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported at the intersection of Noble and Kansas streets in the city of Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating, upslope flow, and a weak upper level disturbance moved across southeastern New Mexico and the northern Permian Basin and combined to produce scattered thunderstorms. A few storms moved at a slow pace, resulting in flash flooding of urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement barricaded roads throughout the city of Jal due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper impulse on the east side of an upper high moved southward into a very moist and unstable air mass over southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew down a few trees in Alamogordo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in northwest flow aloft around high pressure centered over southern New Mexico triggered severe thunderstorms that produced large hail across parts of eastern New Mexico.  The hail caused significant damage in Union County, to the west and northwest of Clayton.

A short wave trough in northwest flow aloft around high pressure centered over southern New Mexico triggered severe thunderstorms that produced large hail across parts of eastern New Mexico.  The hail caused significant damage in Union County, to the west and northwest of Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail damaged several vehicles at Clayton Lake State Park and cracked a window at the visitor center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass settled over southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 3 inches of rain produced flooding which lead to the closure of two roads in the Bayard area. A handful of businesses were damaged, Cobre High School was 3 feet deep in water, and one family was displaced (aided by the local Red Cross) when the foundation of their house cracked. A water rescue was performed in nearby Santa Clara.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in northwest flow aloft around high pressure centered over southern New Mexico triggered severe thunderstorms that produced large hail across parts of eastern New Mexico.  The hail caused significant damage in Union County, to the west and northwest of Clayton.

A shortwave trough moved through the subtropical jet stream into west Texas and southeastern New Mexico.  This combined with moderate levels of CAPE and deep layer shear to produce supercell thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell between Tatum and Gladiola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system remained quasi-stationary over portions of southeastern Arizona. Ahead of this upper low, an upper level disturbance moved around the periphery of the low and aided in the development of thunderstorms over southeastern New Mexico overnight. As the disturbance moved across southeastern New Mexico, thunderstorms developed southwestward into portions of southwest Texas. ||Thunderstorms developed into an organized line that moved across much of West Texas producing strong to severe weather across a good portion of the Permian Basin. Rainfall rates of one to two inches per hour also resulted in flash flooding from portions of southeastern New Mexico eastward into the northern Permian Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of at least six inches of water flowing over several streets in the city of Carlsbad. The flooding continued for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across much of western and central New Mexico producing heavy rain and hail. Flash flooding occurred in Capitan and lightning caused damage in Santa Fe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.8 inches was measured in Capitan in one hour. The heavy rain caused flood waters up to 3 feet deep to flow through some streets in Capitan.  Two secondary roads off Highway 380 east of town were washed out as a crest of water raged downstream topping roads near Salado Creek with up to 6 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved east across northern Mexico across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Ahead of this system, a moist and unstable airmass was in place. As temperatures warmed throughout the day, the atmosphere became more unstable. Storms initiated in the Big Bend and across the mountains of southeastern New Mexico and moved north and eastward. |The Midland county warning area saw five recorded tornadoes on the 23rd, four in New Mexico and one in Texas. The four in New Mexico were documented by both trained Skywarn spotters and by a National Weather Service damage survey team. The tornado in Texas was recorded by a television news/weather crew in the area. Baseball size hail was also associated with the Texas storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to golfball size hail fell in Queen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across much of western and central New Mexico producing heavy rain and hail. Flash flooding occurred in Capitan and lightning caused damage in Santa Fe.
Thunderstorms moved west across much of western and central New Mexico producing heavy rain and hail. Flash flooding occurred in Capitan and lightning caused damage in Santa Fe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree in the yard of a Santa Fe residence.  The shock wave from the strike shattered two windows in the house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous people in Carlsbad reported flash flooding.  Some of the reports include four to six inches of water flowing across low lying roads at the beginning of the evening to three to four feet of water in major intersections in Carlsbad by the end of the night.  Black River Road, Ogden Road, and Higby Hole Road in the Loving/Malaga area were barricaded due to flood waters.  Illinois Camp Road in Carlsbad was declared impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 foot tall and three foot wide Elm tree was blown over. No other damage was reported.

One to two feet of water flowed over New Mexico Highway 137 from its intersection with U.S. Highway 285 for six miles west.

An upper low crossed from northwest to east central New Mexico while a surface low intensified over the Texas Panhandle.  Moderate snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 5 inches from the afternoon of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd.  The snow was blown about by strong northerly winds which created drifts of 4 to 6 feet in mountainous areas.  Visibilities in blowing snow were reduced to near zero at times, especially across the northeast plains.

Lifting of moist and unstable air was aided by 50 knot coupled upper level jet maxima. The resultant thunderstorms moved from the northwest into east central portions of the Albuquerque metro area.  The largest hail reported was generally penny to nickel size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-sized hail was reported at the intersection of Academy and Eubank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough of low pressure crossed New Mexico in two stages, bringing periods of heavy snow to northern and western New Mexico.  Around 4 inches of snow fell between 6500 and 7500 feet with 6 to 12 inches common above 7500 feet.

A broad trough of low pressure crossed New Mexico in two stages, bringing periods of heavy snow to northern and western New Mexico.  Around 4 inches of snow fell between 6500 and 7500 feet with 6 to 12 inches common above 7500 feet.

A storm off the Baja California coast on November 21st and 22nd began to draw moisture north into New Mexico by the 23rd. The remnants of the storm crossed the state on the 24th. This increase in moisture and instability led to a widespread snow event over northern and central New Mexico on the 23rd into the 24th. Hardest hit areas were the central mountain chain onto the adjacent eastern slopes and high plains, the northeast plains, and around Roswell. Snow amounts were as high as 13 inches in portions of the northern mountains, 4 to 7 inches in the central mountains and adjacent high plains, 5 to 6 inches in the south central mountains, 7 to 11 inches in Chaves county including around Roswell, and 3 to 5 inches in the northeast plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eleven inches of snow fell in and around Roswell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather continued on the 4th with deep moisture and strong instability remaining over central and eastern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down at Mile Marker 16 on Highway 246 for 1-2 minutes. Large trees were uprooted and power poles were snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather continued on the 4th with deep moisture and strong instability remaining over central and eastern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather continued on the 4th with deep moisture and strong instability remaining over central and eastern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flag pole was bent and nearly destroyed. One and a half inch diameter branches from elm tree were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong storm moved north across Colorado on the 6th, with a|tight pressure gradient to the south of the storm. This resulted in widespread strong winds over northern and central New Mexico, with numerous reports of strong non thunderstorm winds and isolated damage|reports.

An unusually strong storm moved north across Colorado on the 6th, with a|tight pressure gradient to the south of the storm. This resulted in widespread strong winds over northern and central New Mexico, with numerous reports of strong non thunderstorm winds and isolated damage|reports.

Deep low level moisture and strong instability continued over primarily eastern New Mexico in early June. An upper level disturbance helped trigger a significant outbreak of large hail, along with a tornado and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surge of low level moisture moved into the state late on the 8th and early on the 9th, thanks in part to a strong back door cold front. The front moved south across the easter plains on the 8th and rushed through the central mountain passes into the Rio Grande Valley early on the 9th. The moisture pressed all the way back to the continental divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred in La Bajada, along the Santa Fe and Sandoval County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low level moisture and strong instability continued over primarily eastern New Mexico in early June. An upper level disturbance helped trigger a significant outbreak of large hail, along with a tornado and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported. The tornado stirred up grass and dust near Ocate Crater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low level moisture and strong instability continued over primarily eastern New Mexico in early June. An upper level disturbance helped trigger a significant outbreak of large hail, along with a tornado and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down for about 90 seconds. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather continued on the 4th with deep moisture and strong instability remaining over central and eastern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and windows were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received from the general public and co-op observers that ranged between 2 and 7 inches of snow below 7500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture, daytime heating and decent shear led to isolated to scattered thunderstorms over northeast New Mexico on the 2nd. There was one CoCoRaHS report of half dollar size hail near Springer in south central Colfax county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many vehicles were damaged by the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved southwest from the Texas Panhandle into east central New Mexico and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn door was blown off its hinges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced very large hail across far northeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was 1.25 inches in diameter with a few stones ranging between 2.50 and 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain at a rate between 1 and 2 inches per hour in Albuquerque, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain on the east side of Albuquerque.  One and a half inches of rain was reported at Constitution and Wyoming. Two apartments were flooded and evacuated at 8500 Marquette NE in the Northeast Heights.

This helped spark plenty of showers and thunderstorms over the eastern three quarters of the state. heavy rain, strong winds and hail were reported in these areas, with the|largest hail focused over central New Mexico.
Deep moisture and instability with decent shear continued over eastern New Mexico in early June, resulting in several reports of large hail across the far eastern plains.

The storms began in the early afternoon over the northeast, and advanced south later in the afternoon and evening. The severe weather was initially in the form of large hail, but evolved into a significant high wind event for east central and southeast portions of the state, as an outflow boundary raced south ahead of the main line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The windows were blown out of a commercial building. Power lines and poles were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surge of low level moisture moved into the state late on the 8th and early on the 9th, thanks in part to a strong back door cold front. The front moved south across the easter plains on the 8th and rushed through the central mountain passes into the Rio Grande Valley early on the 9th. The moisture pressed all the way back to the continental divide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was first spotted 2 miles south of Domingo in extreme eastern Sandoval county near Interstate 25. It continued east toward county line, crossing into Santa Fe County around 3:45 pm. The observer at the Santa Fe Municipal Airport reported seeing the tornado for 15 minutes, from 3:27 - 3:42 pm. It's possible then the tornado (landspout) was on the ground before the first report of it 2 miles south of Domingo. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small human caused wildfire which began in the southern Manzano Mountains early in the morning on the 19th grew to around 7000 acres early on the 21st.  Three residences and 4 outbuildings were destroyed.  Nearly 100 people were evacuated prior to Thanksgiving Day in the villages of Punta de Agua and Manzano.

An upper low crossed from northwest to east central New Mexico while a surface low intensified over the Texas Panhandle.  Moderate snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 5 inches from the afternoon of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd.  The snow was blown about by strong northerly winds which created drifts of 4 to 6 feet in mountainous areas.  Visibilities in blowing snow were reduced to near zero at times, especially across the northeast plains.

A storm off the Baja California coast on November 21st and 22nd began to draw moisture north into New Mexico by the 23rd. The remnants of the storm crossed the state on the 24th. This increase in moisture and instability led to a widespread snow event over northern and central New Mexico on the 23rd into the 24th. Hardest hit areas were the central mountain chain onto the adjacent eastern slopes and high plains, the northeast plains, and around Roswell. Snow amounts were as high as 13 inches in portions of the northern mountains, 4 to 7 inches in the central mountains and adjacent high plains, 5 to 6 inches in the south central mountains, 7 to 11 inches in Chaves county including around Roswell, and 3 to 5 inches in the northeast plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six reports were received from co-op observers ranging from 2.5 to 12.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough of low pressure crossed New Mexico in two stages, bringing periods of heavy snow to northern and western New Mexico.  Around 4 inches of snow fell between 6500 and 7500 feet with 6 to 12 inches common above 7500 feet.

A mid level short wave trough and upper level jet maximum acted on very unstable air to enhance severe thunderstorms that produced large hail across northeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail broke two windows in a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which resulted in flooding of several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued heavy rain resulted in flooding of three additional homes, along with substantial street flooding. Water inside of homes was reported at 1 to 2 feet deep.

A vigorous upper low moved southeast across New Mexico while a strong cold front plunged south across the eastern plains.  A narrow band of moderate to heavy snow and blowing snow developed during the evening of the 25th, with snowfall of 2 to 5 inches and reduced visibility in blowing snow.  Numerous vehicles went off the roadway along Interstate 40 between Clines Corners and Tucumcari.  Heavy snow was also reported in Fort Sumner.
Sufficient low level moisture within favorable vertical wind shear led to the formation of severe thunderstorms in Grant County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven reports were received, mostly from co-op observers, ranging from 2 to 13 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in northwest flow aloft around high pressure centered over southern New Mexico triggered severe thunderstorms that produced large hail across parts of eastern New Mexico.  The hail caused significant damage in Union County, to the west and northwest of Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general public and law enforcement officials reported car windshields broken by golf ball-sized hail along Highway 56.  A garden and greenhouse were damaged at a nursery and several homes suffered broken windows and skylights. The hail dented kitchen appliances inside one home as it fell through the skylight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lifting of moist and unstable air was aided by 50 knot coupled upper level jet maxima. The resultant thunderstorms moved from the northwest into east central portions of the Albuquerque metro area.  The largest hail reported was generally penny to nickel size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-sized hail was reported at the intersection of Golf Course and Paseo Del Norte in northwest Albuquerque.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Henriette formed in the eastern Pacific Ocean and made landfall along the western coast of Mexico. The storm's remnants moved northeast across Mexico, entering the United States near El Paso. The system brought abundant moisture to west Texas and southeastern New Mexico resulting in flash flooding and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hobbs Police Department reported widespread flooding of city streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Henriette continued to track across west Texas and southeastern New Mexico as it was being absorbed by a cold front moving through New Mexico. The flow of moisture from the Pacific Ocean, moderate instability, and marginal shear contributed to flash flooding and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hobbs Police Department reported widespread flooding of city streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough ejected northeast across New Mexico.  Heavy snow generally between 8 and 16 inches accumulated above 7500 feet in the mountains of McKinley, Rio Arriba, Sandoval and San Juan Counties including the Chuska, Zuni, Southern San Juan and Jemez ranges.

A short wave trough ejected northeast across New Mexico.  Heavy snow generally between 8 and 16 inches accumulated above 7500 feet in the mountains of McKinley, Rio Arriba, Sandoval and San Juan Counties including the Chuska, Zuni, Southern San Juan and Jemez ranges.

An upper level low and associated strong subtropical jet brought heavy rain, high elevation snow, severe thunderstorms and high winds to western and central New Mexico during the predawn hours of December 1st.  Flooding was observed in the Gila region while severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in the Rio Grande and Estancia Valleys. Strong winds blew across much of the high terrain and just east of the central mountain chain but were the strongest during the daylight hours of the 1st. The winds were occasionally accompanied by snow, which created near blizzard conditions in the higher mountainous terrain. As the trough opened up and moved east across New Mexico and Colorado, snow levels lowered overnight across the northwest.  However, the most significant snow accumulations were confined to elevations above 8500 feet in the Chuska, Southern San Juan and Sangre De Cristo Mountains of northern New Mexico. Through dawn of the 2nd, storm total snow accumulations of 5 to 10 inches were common with 1 to 2 feet likely on west facing slopes above 9500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2 to 2.5 inches caused Whitewater Creek to leave its banks and flow across Highway 174, resulting in its closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front slid into northeast New Mexico early on the 10th and acted as a focus for thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Plenty of low level moisture and instability was already in place, along with decent shear through the atmosphere. A few of the thunderstorms over|Mora, San Miguel, Torrance and Guadalupe counties produced hail between the size of nickels and golf balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two tractors were blown off Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening trough of low pressure along the west coast began to draw moisture north into New Mexico early on the 28th. This helped destabilize the atmosphere. In addition, strong directional and speed shear near the surface helped produce long lived storms. The result was several reports of large (0.75 to 1.00 inc) hail over northern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea-sized to penny-sized.

An isolated thunderstorm became severe with damaging wind gusts as it moved through Roswell.  A two foot diameter tree was snapped at the trunk. Other tree limbs were knocked down as were a couple of power lines.
With an upper level high pressure system to the east, the combination of deep easterly flow, instability, and upper level disturbances moving across the area led to thunderstorm development.

A broad high pressure ridge was situated over much of the U.S. The combination of a series of upper level disturbances moving around this ridge and abundant low level moisture provided by southeast flow off the Gulf of Mexico, aided in the development of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain across portions of West Texas and southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three feet of water flowed across Marathon Road west southwest of Lakewood.  Six inches of water was flowing through Canal Street in Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad high pressure ridge was situated over much of the U.S. The combination of a series of upper level disturbances moving around this ridge and abundant low level moisture provided by southeast flow off the Gulf of Mexico, aided in the development of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain across portions of West Texas and southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement barricaded the Rocky Arroyo crossing due to four feet deep water rapidly flowing across New Mexico State Highway 137 near Eddy County 401.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the northern side of a westward moving upper level storm system.  Weak mid and upper level wind fields resulted in slow storm movements, along with heavy rains and flooding across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local police reported six inches to one foot of water across several streets in the city of Artesia.

A landspout tornado formed within a circulation associated with the remnants of former Hurricane Henriette which moved inland from the Gulf of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser photographed a small tornado southwest of Tyrone, NM. It was on the ground for about 15 minutes, but caused no damage as it was in a remote area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper trough moved into a very moist and unstable air mass and triggered thunderstorms which were efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 92 was impassable due to flooded washes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper trough moved into a very moist and unstable air mass and triggered thunderstorms which were efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1.5 inches of rain within 30 minutes resulted in flash flooding in the north portion of Silver City. At least one road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper trough moved into a very moist and unstable air mass and triggered thunderstorms which were efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall resulted in boulders 2 feet in diameter falling on to the access road to Chino Mine, stranding employees for hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance was approaching a very moist and unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from an upstream persistent thunderstorm reached Cliff and affected the high school and some residences. Evacuations were considered but the water level receded in time. Radar estimates, which were assumed to be accurate considering the deep moist layer, were up to 4 inches under the source storm.

Abundant Pacific moisture continued to stream northeastward into west Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Similar to the previous day, a stalled front continued to be the focal point for heavy rainfall. Another contributing factor to the shower activity was a second cold front moving into west Texas from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported several streets in Carlsbad had six inches of water flowing through the intersections.
